title: Accordion fold zine
subtitle: A multi-panel zine that folds flat for mailing and opens into a full panorama
excerpt: An accordion fold zine is a strip of paper folded into alternating mountain and valley folds to create a series of connected panels. It can be read panel by panel or opened fully to reveal a panorama. It mails flat, needs no binding, and can be made in minutes from a single long strip or taped-together A4 sheets.
sourceNotes: The accordion fold is a foundational paper-folding structure used across bookbinding, zine-making, and origami traditions. The application to self-published zines is a 20th-century independent publishing practice. The construction described here is a synthesis of well-established accordion book and zine formats.

content.text: zine has one structural rule: each fold is the opposite of the last.
and valley folds alternate, which is what makes the strip fold flat and then unfold to a standing panorama. The number of

is limited only by the length of the strip. Six or eight panels gives a readable small zine; twelve or more makes a panoramic fold-out. The zine needs no glue to hold its folded form; the folded state is held by the tension of the paper.

items.qty: As long as needed for the panel count
items.name: Paper strip: a single long strip or multiple A4 sheets taped end to end along their short edges
Metal ruler and pencil (for marking panel widths before folding)
Glue stick or PVA (for joining strips, if using multiple sheets)

content.text: An A5 panel (148 mm wide, 210 mm tall) is a common size for a readable small zine. Six panels require a strip about 900 mm long. Decide on panel size first, then cut or join paper to the correct length. Mark each fold line in pencil on the strip before folding.
Score each fold line lightly with the bone folder against the ruler before folding. Scoring ensures clean, sharp folds through the full thickness of the paper.
Fold the first panel up (valley fold): the left end of the strip folds toward you and up.
At the second scored line, fold the opposite direction (mountain fold): the paper folds away from you. Continue alternating directions at each score line to the end of the strip.
Burnish each fold firmly with the bone folder once all the folds are made. The strip should compress flat with all panels stacked.

Content can be added before or after folding. Drawing on the flat strip and then folding is easier for designs that cross panel edges. For text-only panels, content is easier to place if the strip is folded first and then written panel by panel. To add a cover, glue a card slightly larger than the folded panel to the front and back faces of the accordion.
